app.name: GovOps
app.tagline: Law as code, with provenance you can read.
theme.toggle.label: Toggle theme
lang.switcher.label: Language
about.title: About GovOps
about.lede: GovOps treats public rules as living code: drafted by agents, ratified by humans, auditable by citizens.
about.philosophy.title: Statute meets System
about.philosophy.body: Two registers, one platform. The statute register — serif type, parchment, civic gold — carries the weight of human authority. The system register — mono type, ink surfaces, lavender — exposes machine reasoning. Every artifact wears its provenance on its sleeve, so legitimacy is never confused with automation.
about.principles.title: Operating principles
about.principles.provenance: Every clause shows who wrote it.
about.principles.bilingual: Every interface speaks the citizen's language, RTL included.
about.principles.auditability: Every change is diffable, reversible, and signed.
about.ribbons.title: Provenance, at a glance
about.ribbons.caption: A 3px inline-start ribbon labels every artifact. Color is paired with an ARIA label so meaning never relies on sight alone.
about.ribbons.example.agent: Draft proposal generated overnight by the policy agent.
about.ribbons.example.human: Ordinance signed by the regional minister.
about.ribbons.example.hybrid: Agent-drafted clause, ratified by committee vote.
about.ribbons.example.system: Automated compliance check, no human review.
about.ribbons.example.citizen: Public comment submitted via the open portal.

home.lede: GovOps is the agentic, multilingual platform for law-as-code: drafted by agents, ratified by humans, auditable by citizens. Every clause wears its provenance on its sleeve.
home.pillars.title: Three registers, one record
home.pillars.agent.title: Agent-drafted
home.pillars.agent.body: Policy agents propose precise, citable language overnight — never enacted on their own.
home.pillars.human.title: Human-ratified
home.pillars.human.body: Ministers, committees, and councils sign every clause. Authority stays with people.
home.pillars.citizen.title: Citizen-auditable
home.pillars.citizen.body: Every change is diffable, reversible, and signed. The public reads the same record you do.
home.personas.nav.heading: Who is this for?
home.personas.citizens.heading: Read the rules in your language. See the citation. Know the date.
home.personas.citizens.lead: GovOps is open public infrastructure. Every threshold, every accepted document, every recommendation is a dated record with a citation you can read. The same record an officer sees evaluating your case is the record published here. There is nothing in code that isn't on the page.
home.personas.citizens.card1.title: Every parameter has a citation
home.personas.citizens.card1.body: When the minimum age for a benefit changes, the entry shows the statute, the date it took effect, and who ratified it. No buried logic.
home.personas.citizens.card2.title: Decisions trace to source
home.personas.citizens.card2.body: A case decision links each evaluated rule to the legislative text it implements. You can follow the chain back to the constitution.
home.personas.citizens.card3.title: Changes are temporal, not destructive
home.personas.citizens.card3.body: When a rule changes, the prior version stays in the public record. A case evaluated last year remains reproducible against the rules in force then.
home.personas.citizens.cta.secondary: See how a case is decided
home.personas.servants.heading: Configure without deploy. Every decision provenanced. Authority stays with you.
home.personas.servants.lead: GovOps is operational software for the people who actually adjudicate. The agent proposes; you decide. Threshold changes, evidence rules, even the LLM prompts that read legislation are dated records that go through your approvals queue — not a code release.
home.personas.servants.card1.title: Change a threshold without a deploy
home.personas.servants.card1.body: File a draft with citation and rationale; a peer reviews; the new value takes effect on the date you set. The system writes the entry, not your DBA.
home.personas.servants.card2.title: Every recommendation cites its rule
home.personas.servants.card2.body: When the engine returns ineligible, the audit lists which rule fired, which parameter version it used, and which evidence types it expected. Defensible to ombudsman, courts, and committees.
home.personas.servants.card3.title: AI proposes; you ratify
home.personas.servants.card3.body: Encoder agents read legislation and draft rule entries. They land in the same approvals queue you already use. Nothing skips the human signature.
home.personas.leaders.heading: Reduce policy-to-implementation lag. Procurement-ready audit. No vendor lock-in.
home.personas.leaders.lead: GovOps is the open foundation for jurisdiction-as-code. Apache 2.0, schema-published, federation-ready. Adopt it incrementally — one program, one jurisdiction, one prompt at a time. We are not selling a SaaS. We are publishing a standard you can deploy, audit, and contribute to.
home.personas.leaders.card1.title: Policy-to-service lag becomes a configuration write
home.personas.leaders.card1.body: The bottleneck between law passing and the service reflecting it shrinks from months to a review cycle. The substrate was built to read policy, not be patched per legislation.
home.personas.leaders.card2.title: Audit-ready by construction
home.personas.leaders.card2.body: Every artefact traces from constitution to citizen decision. ATO, SOC, ombudsman, parliamentary committee — all see the same chain of evidence. JSON Schema is published and CI-validated.
home.personas.leaders.card3.title: Cross-jurisdiction reusability
home.personas.leaders.card3.body: A province publishes its rules; the federal layer fetches them with signed manifests and checksum-pinned commits. Vendor-neutral — no proprietary registry between you and the source.
home.personas.leaders.engage.heading: Three concrete ways to engage
home.personas.leaders.engage.option1.bod: Fund domain experts to encode an existing program in your country. Output is a YAML pull request, not a vendor invoice.
home.personas.leaders.engage.option2.bod: Deploy the substrate against a single benefit. Measure the policy-to-service lag reduction empirically before expanding scope.
home.personas.leaders.engage.option3.bod: For sectors where multiple agencies share rules — pension, immigration, tax — pay for the signing infrastructure that keeps cross-source provenance honest.
home.preview.title: Live ConfigValue registry
home.preview.caption: A snapshot of the statute keys currently in force. Open the registry to filter by jurisdiction, domain, and language.
home.footnote: Drafted by agents · ratified by humans · auditable by citizens

home.modules.title: What's inside
home.modules.lead: Seven modules compose the platform. Each is a small, single-purpose surface — runnable on its own, valuable in combination, and replaceable without rewriting the rest.
home.modules.substrate.title: Substrate
home.modules.substrate.body: Dated, citable ConfigValue records — every threshold, accepted document type, UI label, and prompt. The single source of truth the engine resolves against.
home.modules.encoder.title: Encoder
home.modules.encoder.body: Agent-drafted rule proposals from legislative text. The agent reads the statute and writes structured records; humans never lose authorship.
home.modules.engine.title: Engine
home.modules.engine.body: Deterministic rule evaluation. Same inputs, same outputs, every time. Each decision links the rule that fired to the citation that backed it.
home.modules.approvals.title: Approvals
home.modules.approvals.body: Draft → review → ratify state machine for every ConfigValue change. Two-person rule available; every transition is logged.
home.modules.audit.title: Audit
home.modules.audit.body: Every resolution, every approval, every decision recorded with actor, timestamp, and the version of every record that participated.
home.modules.schema.title: Schema
home.modules.schema.body: JSON Schema for the substrate, published and CI-validated. Non-Python contributors edit YAML and the build tells them whether it's valid.
home.modules.federation.title: Federation
home.modules.federation.body: Cross-jurisdiction sourcing with signed manifests and checksum-pinned commits. A province publishes; the federal layer fetches; provenance stays honest.
home.actors.title: Who does what
home.actors.lead: Four actors keep the loop honest. Each contributes a register of authorship; none can act for another.
home.actors.agents.title: Encoder agents
home.actors.agents.body: Read legislation, propose structured rules, draft prompts. They prepare; they do not enact. Every output lands in a human's queue.
home.actors.servants.title: Public servants
home.actors.servants.body: Ratify proposals, set effective dates, sign citations. They keep the authority of decision-making — the system writes the entry only after they approve.
home.actors.citizens.title: Citizens
home.actors.citizens.body: Read the public record in their language. Trace any decision to its rule, any rule to its citation, any change to its date. File cases and follow them.
home.actors.leaders.title: Leaders
home.actors.leaders.body: Government, private sector, foundations. Sponsor encodings, fund federated trust, deploy pilots. Adopt incrementally — Apache 2.0, no vendor lock-in.
home.walkthroughCta.heading: A new benefit law passes — what happens next?
home.walkthroughCta.body: Walk through a hypothetical Paid Statutory Vacation Act from royal assent to the first applicants — including the edge case that gets denied with a readable citation.
walkthrough.heading: How a brand-new statutory benefit becomes service
walkthrough.lead: A government has just passed a new benefit law. This walkthrough takes it from the gazette to the first applicants. Each step is the same path the live console exercises — the data shown here mirrors what an operator and a citizen would actually see.
walkthrough.scenario.title: The scenario
walkthrough.scenario.body: The Paid Statutory Vacation Act grants 5 consecutive business days of paid leave. Eligibility: applicants must show 5 consecutive years with the same employer, the employer must be a registered business, and pay rate is established by submitted pay stubs.
walkthrough.step.label: step {num}
walkthrough.step1.heading: The law is enacted
walkthrough.step1.body: The legislative text is published. Citizens can read it; engines cannot. The job from here is to turn this prose into something an engine can evaluate without ever losing the link back to this text.
walkthrough.step1.excerpt.label: statutory excerpt (illustrative)
walkthrough.step1.excerpt.title: Paid Statutory Vacation Act, 2026
walkthrough.step1.excerpt.s3: An eligible person is entitled to five (5) consecutive business days of paid statutory leave per calendar year, paid by the Minister.
walkthrough.step1.excerpt.s4_1: A person is eligible if they have completed at least five (5) consecutive years of employment with the same employer at the date of application.
walkthrough.step1.excerpt.s4_2: The employer must be a business registered under the Business Registration Act.
walkthrough.step1.excerpt.r7: Acceptable evidence of employment and pay rate includes T4 slips, pay stubs, and signed letters of employment from the registered employer.
walkthrough.step2.heading: An agent reads it and proposes structured rules
walkthrough.step2.body: The encoder agent ingests the statutory text and emits draft ConfigValue records — one per testable parameter. Each proposal cites the exact section it came from. The agent does not enact anything; the proposals land in a queue.
walkthrough.step2.proposals.label: agent proposals · pending review
walkthrough.step3.heading: A domain expert ratifies each proposal
walkthrough.step3.body: The proposals appear in the approvals queue. A reviewer can accept, edit (with rationale), or reject. Edits are themselves audit events. The two-person rule is available for high-impact records — author and approver must be distinct.
walkthrough.step3.queue.label: approvals queue · 4 pending
walkthrough.step3.queue.afterReview: After review, the four records below transition to status=approved with the reviewer's signature attached. The audit table records each event.
walkthrough.step4.heading: ConfigValues land in the substrate, dated and citable
walkthrough.step4.body: The approved records become the engine's source of truth. Each is timestamped, citation-linked, and immutable in place — future changes are supersessions with new effective dates, not destructive edits.
walkthrough.step4.records.label: substrate · approved on 2026-04-26
walkthrough.step4.records.schemaNote: Each record satisfies schema/configvalue-v1.0.json. CI runs the validator on every push so a malformed entry fails the build before merge.
walkthrough.step5.heading: First applicant arrives — Maria Santos
walkthrough.step5.body: Maria has eight years at Café Solar Inc., a registered business. She submits a T4 and a pay stub. The engine evaluates each rule against the substrate as of today. Each check passes; the recommendation is eligible. Five days of paid leave are approved.
walkthrough.step6.heading: Edge case — Carlos Mendes
walkthrough.step6.body: Carlos has four years at Acme Logistics — also a registered business — and submits full evidence. One rule fails: the consecutive-employment threshold needs five years, he has four. The recommendation is ineligible.
walkthrough.step6.note: The decision page shows the exact rule that failed and the citation that backed it (s. 4(1) of the Act). Carlos can reapply once he reaches five years; the case audit notes the earliest reapplication date.
walkthrough.step7.heading: Both decisions enter the public record
walkthrough.step7.body: Every event — the law passing, the encoder proposals, the approvals, the two cases — is in the audit log with an actor, a timestamp, and the version of every record that participated. Anyone can re-resolve the rules as of the evaluation date and reproduce the decision exactly.
walkthrough.step7.audit.label: audit log · paid statutory vacation cohort
walkthrough.step7.audit.evt1: 2026-04-26 · law-enacted · Parliament — Paid Statutory Vacation Act receives royal assent.
walkthrough.step7.audit.evt4: 2026-04-27 · case-decided · case-2026-001 · Maria Santos · eligible · 5 days approved.
walkthrough.step7.audit.evt5: 2026-04-27 · case-decided · case-2026-002 · Carlos Mendes · ineligible · cited s. 4(1) · reapply on or after 2027-08-14.
walkthrough.closing.heading: Now do it for real
walkthrough.closing.body: The same path runs against the live console. File a draft, watch it land in the queue, approve it, and resolve a ConfigValue at a future evaluation date — the change takes effect when you said it would, and the audit records every step.
breadcrumb.label: Breadcrumb
dataflow.label: GovOps data flow
dataflow.eyebrow: data flow · the path of one law
dataflow.caption: Seven stages from a law passing to a citizen-facing decision and into the public audit. Each stage carries a distinct register of authorship — agent, human, system, or citizen — and never crosses lanes silently.
dataflow.legend.label: Provenance register legend
dataflow.legend.title: register
dataflow.stage.law.title: Law passes
dataflow.stage.law.body: Legislature publishes statutory text. Citation lives at the source.
dataflow.stage.encoder.title: Encoder reads it
dataflow.stage.encoder.body: Agent drafts structured rule proposals — never enacts.
dataflow.stage.review.title: Reviewer ratifies
dataflow.stage.review.body: Domain expert accepts, edits with rationale, or rejects.
dataflow.stage.substrate.title: Substrate stores it
dataflow.stage.substrate.body: Dated, citable, schema-validated. The engine resolves against this.
dataflow.stage.engine.title: Engine evaluates
dataflow.stage.engine.body: Deterministic. Same inputs, same outputs. Citations follow each rule that fired.
dataflow.stage.case.title: Citizen receives a decision
dataflow.stage.case.body: Recommendation links to rules, rules to citations. Reproducible.
dataflow.stage.audit.title: Audit publishes the record
dataflow.stage.audit.body: Every event with actor, timestamp, and the version of every record that participated.
help.button.label: Help
help.section.dataflow: Where you are in the flow
help.section.dataflow.body: GovOps moves a single law through seven stages. The diagram below shows the full path; this screen sits inside one of those stages.
help.tip.breadcrumb: Breadcrumbs at the top of every drill-down page show the path back to the parent surface. The Console dropdown in the navigation lists all operational surfaces in one place.
help.home.title: Home
help.home.summary: The front door of the platform. Reads the idea, names the modules and actors, and points you at a concrete walkthrough or the operational console.
help.home.bullet1: Skim the three pillars (agent-drafted · human-ratified · citizen-auditable) to understand the authority model.
help.home.bullet2: Read the modules section to see the seven components — substrate, encoder, engine, approvals, audit, schema, federation.
help.home.bullet3: Open the walkthrough to see a hypothetical law travel from gazette to first applicants.
help.home.bullet4: Use the Console dropdown to jump into operational surfaces (Cases, Encode, Config, Approvals, Prompts).
help.walkthrough.title: Walkthrough — paid statutory vacation
help.walkthrough.summary: An end-to-end story showing how a brand-new benefit law becomes service: encode, ratify, store in the substrate, evaluate cases, write the audit.
help.walkthrough.bullet1: Read the seven steps top-to-bottom — each section has its own data preview shaped like the live console.
help.walkthrough.bullet2: Step 5 (Maria) shows the eligible path; step 6 (Carlos) shows the edge case denial with a readable citation.
help.walkthrough.bullet3: Each step's CTA opens the matching live page so you can do the same thing on real data.
help.walkthrough.bullet4: The closing block points you at the approvals queue to file a real draft against the live substrate.
help.authority.title: Authority chain
help.authority.summary: Visualization of the lineage that backs every decision: constitution → law → regulation → policy → service. Click into a node to see the artefact and the rules that depend on it.
help.authority.bullet1: Use this view to answer: which legislative source drives a given threshold?
help.authority.bullet2: Reverse-impact: which rules cite a particular statute? Useful when an act is amended.
help.authority.bullet3: Each node shows the jurisdiction; the chain tolerates federation across multiple sources.
help.about.title: About GovOps
help.about.summary: Philosophy, scope, and what GovOps is not. Useful for stakeholders deciding whether to adopt or contribute.
help.about.bullet1: GovOps is decision support — humans always sign the act of decision.
help.about.bullet2: Apache 2.0, no vendor lock-in. Adopt incrementally.
help.about.bullet3: Policy text is for illustration only; nothing here is authoritative operational guidance.
help.policies.title: Policies
help.policies.summary: Reference catalog of policies relevant to the platform's operation: licensing, contribution, code of conduct, citation standards.
help.policies.bullet1: Read the licensing notes before forking or redistributing.
help.policies.bullet2: Citation policy explains how to reference statutes you encode.
help.policies.bullet3: Contribution checklist: open an ADR for any architectural decision.
help.cases.title: Cases
help.cases.summary: Triage queue and detail pages for live and historical applications. Each case carries its evidence, recommendation, and audit package.
help.cases.bullet1: Filter by status to focus on what needs your attention.
help.cases.bullet2: Open a case to see the rules evaluated, with each citation linked.
help.cases.bullet3: Use the audit drawer to see every evaluation that has run on this case.
help.cases.bullet4: The runbook below covers triage, evaluation, and decision flows step by step.
help.encode.title: Encoder
help.encode.summary: Pipeline for turning legislative text into ConfigValue proposals. Agent-drafted; you ratify each entry before it lands.
help.encode.bullet1: Open an existing batch to review proposals from a prior extraction run.
help.encode.bullet2: Start a new extraction by pasting statutory text — the agent handles parsing and proposal generation.
help.encode.bullet3: Each proposal cites the section it came from; you can edit before approving.
help.encode.bullet4: Commit a batch to push approved proposals to the approvals queue.
help.config.title: Configuration registry
help.config.summary: Search and browse the substrate. Every threshold, accepted document type, UI label, and prompt is here as a dated, citable record.
help.config.bullet1: Use the filters to scope by domain, jurisdiction, language, or status.
help.config.bullet2: Click into a key to see its full version history and supersession chain.
help.config.bullet3: Compare any two versions side-by-side using the diff view.
help.config.bullet4: Draft new values via the Draft tab — every change goes through approvals.
help.configDraft.title: Draft a ConfigValue
help.configDraft.summary: Form to propose a new ConfigValue or supersede an existing one. Citation and rationale are mandatory; effective_from controls when the change takes effect.
help.configDraft.bullet1: Pick the key shape carefully — per-parameter granularity (one record per leaf value).
help.configDraft.bullet2: Citation should point at the exact section authorising the change.
help.configDraft.bullet3: Set effective_from to a future date if the rule kicks in later.
help.configDraft.bullet4: Submit puts the draft in the approvals queue; nothing is enacted until ratified.
help.configApprovals.title: Approvals queue
help.configApprovals.summary: Pending and recent ConfigValue drafts awaiting human ratification. Each action — approve, request changes, reject — is logged with your name and the timestamp.
help.configApprovals.bullet1: Filter by status (draft / pending) to triage your inbox.
help.configApprovals.bullet2: Open a draft to see what's changing relative to the current value.
help.configApprovals.bullet3: Approving requires you not be the author (two-person rule).
help.configApprovals.bullet4: Rejecting is terminal — record the reason in the comment for the audit.
help.configPrompts.title: Prompt admin
help.configPrompts.summary: LLM prompts as ConfigValues. Each prompt is dated and citable; encoder runs pin the prompt id used so old extractions stay reproducible.
help.configPrompts.bullet1: Browse prompts by domain — extraction, audit summary, officer explanation.
help.configPrompts.bullet2: Edit a prompt with the dedicated editor; live preview shows variable substitution.
help.configPrompts.bullet3: Test against a fixture batch before approving — verifies the prompt extracts what you expect.
help.configPrompts.bullet4: Per ADR-008, prompt changes require dual approval — author and approver must be distinct.
help.configDiff.title: Diff view
help.configDiff.summary: Side-by-side comparison of two ConfigValue versions. Useful for understanding what changed when, and which policy decision drove the change.
help.configDiff.bullet1: Pick two versions from the timeline to compare.
help.configDiff.bullet2: Citations and rationales appear alongside the values for context.
help.configDiff.bullet3: Print the diff for an offline case file or hearing record.
help.admin.title: Admin
help.admin.summary: Operator tooling for jurisdiction onboarding, demo seeding, and platform configuration.
help.admin.bullet1: Switch the active demo jurisdiction to test rules in another country's context.
help.admin.bullet2: Reseed the demo data — useful when iterating on changes.
help.admin.bullet3: Onboard a new jurisdiction by following the domain-expert checklist.
runbook.config.title: ConfigValue registry runbook
runbook.config.lead: Three scenarios that cover most operator interactions with the substrate.
runbook.config.find.title: Find a parameter
runbook.config.find.step2: Type a partial key in the search box; results filter as you type.
runbook.config.find.step3: Click the row to open the full version history and citations.
runbook.config.find.step4: If nothing matches, switch the language filter — UI labels are scoped per locale.
runbook.config.history.title: Trace a parameter's history
runbook.config.history.step1: From the registry, click into the key you want to inspect.
runbook.config.history.step2: The timeline shows every supersession with effective dates and approvers.
runbook.config.history.step3: Select two versions and open Diff to see exactly what changed.
runbook.config.draft.title: Propose a change
runbook.config.draft.step1: Open the Draft tab and pick the key (or supersede an existing record).
runbook.config.draft.step2: Fill in the new value, citation, rationale, and effective_from.
runbook.config.draft.step3: Save the draft — it appears in the approvals queue.
runbook.config.draft.step4: A reviewer (different from you) ratifies it.
runbook.config.draft.step5: On approval, the new value resolves on or after effective_from. Old cases stay reproducible against the prior version.
runbook.cases.title: Case-handling runbook
runbook.cases.lead: From triage to decision — the operator's path through a single case file.
runbook.cases.triage.title: Triage a new case
runbook.cases.triage.step1: From the queue, filter by status "intake" to see new arrivals.
runbook.cases.triage.step2: Open each case and confirm the applicant fields and submitted evidence are complete.
runbook.cases.triage.step3: Run the engine — recommendations populate from the substrate.
runbook.cases.triage.step4: If evidence is missing, mark the case as "info-needed" and notify the applicant.
runbook.cases.evaluate.title: Evaluate a case
runbook.cases.evaluate.step1: On the case detail page, the recommendation pane lists each rule and its outcome.
runbook.cases.evaluate.step2: Each rule cites the statutory section that authorises it.
runbook.cases.evaluate.step3: If a rule's parameter has changed since the application, the engine still uses the version in force on the evaluation date.
runbook.cases.evaluate.step4: Click into the audit drawer to see every prior evaluation on this case.
runbook.cases.evaluate.step5: Confirm the recommendation matches your reading of the statute before deciding.
runbook.cases.decide.title: Record the decision
runbook.cases.decide.step1: The Review pane offers approve, modify, reject, or request-info actions.
runbook.cases.decide.step2: If you modify, log a rationale that survives in the audit.
runbook.cases.decide.step3: Submit; the case status updates and the applicant sees the outcome.
runbook.cases.decide.step4: If new evidence arrives later, post a life-event and the case re-evaluates.
runbook.encode.title: Encoder runbook
runbook.encode.lead: How to turn a freshly-passed statute into ratified ConfigValue records.
runbook.encode.ingest.title: Ingest a statute
runbook.encode.ingest.step1: Open New extraction and paste the legislative text.
runbook.encode.ingest.step2: Pick the jurisdiction and the prompt template (extraction defaults to the system prompt).
runbook.encode.ingest.step3: Run the extraction — the agent emits proposals one per testable parameter.
runbook.encode.ingest.step4: Each proposal cites the exact section it came from.
runbook.encode.review.title: Review proposals
runbook.encode.review.step1: Open the batch and read each proposal alongside the source text.
runbook.encode.review.step2: Approve, edit (with rationale), or reject each one individually.
runbook.encode.review.step3: Use bulk-review when many proposals are obviously correct.
runbook.encode.review.step4: If the agent missed a clause, file a follow-up extraction or a manual draft.
runbook.encode.review.step5: Edits become audit events — the original proposal and your change are both kept.
runbook.encode.commit.title: Commit the batch
runbook.encode.commit.step1: When all proposals are reviewed, click Commit.
runbook.encode.commit.step2: Approved proposals push to the approvals queue for a final ratification.
runbook.encode.commit.step3: On approval, they land in the substrate and the engine starts using them on or after effective_from.
